question.text: Scenario: Ankit Sharma is a 28-year-old single software engineer working at an IT company in Bengaluru. His CTC is Rs. 15,00,000 per annum with a monthly take-home pay of Rs. 95,000. His monthly expenses are Rs. 45,000, leaving him with monthly savings of Rs. 50,000. He has no outstanding loans. Ankit has the following financial goals: (1) Build an emergency fund covering 6 months of expenses (immediate priority), (2) Accumulate Rs. 15 lakh for a house down payment in 3 years, (3) Save Rs. 10 lakh for marriage expenses in 5 years, and (4) Build a retirement corpus (he plans to retire at 60, giving him a 32-year horizon). His risk profile has been assessed as Moderately Aggressive. Currently, Ankit has Rs. 2 lakh lying in a savings account as his only investment. His investment adviser is helping him compare various products for each goal. Question 1 of 5: For Ankit's emergency fund goal of 6 months' expenses (Rs. 2,70,000), which product is MOST suitable?

answer.text: ELSS mutual fund for tax savings along with emergency coverage
Public Provident Fund for guaranteed returns and safety
A combination of savings account and liquid/ultra-short duration debt fund for instant to next-day liquidity
Tax-saving bank fixed deposit for assured returns

explanation.text: **The Logic:** An emergency fund must prioritize liquidity and capital preservation above all else. A combination of savings account (for instant access) and liquid/ultra-short duration fund (for next-day redemption with slightly better returns) is ideal. Ankit already has Rs. 2 lakh in savings, so he only needs to add Rs. 70,000 more to reach his 6-month target of Rs. 2,70,000. **The Trap:** ELSS has a 3-year lock-in making it completely unsuitable for emergencies. PPF has a 15-year lock-in with partial withdrawal only after 5 years. Tax-saving FDs have a 5-year lock-in. None of these provide the instant liquidity that an emergency fund demands. **Cross-Exam Context:** NISM Series V-A covers liquid fund features; NISM Series X-A covers emergency fund planning as part of financial planning basics. **Distributor's Pro-Tip:** Always build the emergency fund FIRST before allocating to any other goal. Ankit's existing Rs. 2 lakh in savings account is a good start -- he just needs to top up Rs. 70,000 in a liquid fund. Only after this safety net is in place should he start investing for other goals.
